* [Zeuhl](/wiki/Zeuhl "Zeuhl")[[1]](#cite_note-1) * [smooth jazz](/wiki/Smooth_jazz "Smooth jazz") * [acid jazz](/wiki/Acid_jazz "Acid jazz") * [quiet storm](/wiki/Quiet_storm "Quiet storm") * [urban contemporary](/wiki/Urban_contemporary "Urban contemporary") * [nu jazz](/wiki/Nu_jazz "Nu jazz") * [technical death metal](/wiki/Technical_death_metal "Technical death metal")[[2]](#cite_note-2)

(also known as **jazz rock**, **jazz-rock fusion**, or simply **fusion**[[3]](#cite_note-3)) is a [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") [genre](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre") that developed in the late 1960s when musicians combined [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") harmony and [improvisation](/wiki/Jazz_improvisation "Jazz improvisation") with [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), and [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"). [Electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") and [basses](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar"), [amplifiers](/wiki/Guitar_amplifier "Guitar amplifier"), and [keyboard instruments](/wiki/Keyboard_instrument "Keyboard instrument") (including [electric pianos](/wiki/Electric_piano "Electric piano") and [organs](/wiki/Electric_organ "Electric organ")) that were popular in rock began to be used by jazz musicians, particularly those who had grown up listening to rock and roll.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Music genre combining jazz methods with rock music, funk, and rhythm and blues

| Cultural origins | Late 1960s, United States |

**Jazz fusion** (also known as **jazz rock**, **jazz-rock fusion**, or simply **fusion**[[3]](#cite_note-3)) is a [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") [genre](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre") that developed in the late 1960s when musicians combined [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") harmony and [improvisation](/wiki/Jazz_improvisation "Jazz improvisation") with [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), and [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"). [Electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") and [basses](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar"), [amplifiers](/wiki/Guitar_amplifier "Guitar amplifier"), and [keyboard instruments](/wiki/Keyboard_instrument "Keyboard instrument") (including [electric pianos](/wiki/Electric_piano "Electric piano") and [organs](/wiki/Electric_organ "Electric organ")) that were popular in rock began to be used by jazz musicians, particularly those who had grown up listening to rock and roll.

Jazz fusion [arrangements](/wiki/Arrangements "Arrangements") vary in complexity. Some employ groove-based vamps fixed to a single key or a single chord with a simple, repeated melody. Others use elaborate [chord progressions](/wiki/Chord_progression "Chord progression"), unconventional time signatures, or melodies with [counter-melodies](/wiki/Hauptstimme "Hauptstimme"), in a similar fashion to [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock"). These arrangements, whether simple or complex, typically include improvised sections that can vary in length, much like in other forms of jazz.

As with jazz, jazz fusion can employ brass and woodwind instruments such as trumpet and saxophone, but other instruments often substitute for these. A jazz fusion band is less likely to use acoustic [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano") and [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass"), and more likely to use electric guitars, electric pianos, organs, [synthesizers](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer"), and [bass guitar](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar").

The term "jazz rock" is sometimes used as a synonym for "jazz fusion" and for music performed by late 1960s- and 1970s-era rock bands that added jazz elements to their music. After a decade of popularity during the 1970s, fusion expanded its improvisatory and experimental approaches through the 1980s in parallel with the development of a radio-friendly style called [smooth jazz](/wiki/Smooth_jazz "Smooth jazz").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Experimentation continued in the 1990s and 2000s. Fusion albums, even those that are made by the same group or artist, may include a variety of musical styles. Rather than being a codified musical style, fusion can be viewed as a musical tradition or approach.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

When [John Coltrane](/wiki/John_Coltrane "John Coltrane") died in 1967, rock was the most popular music in America, and *[DownBeat](/wiki/DownBeat "DownBeat")* magazine went so far as to declare in a headline that: "Jazz as We Know It Is Dead".[[5]](#cite_note-Fusions-5) [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") states that "until around 1967, the worlds of jazz and rock were nearly completely separate",[[6]](#cite_note-Fusion_overview-6) although the [Daevid Allen Trio](/wiki/Soft_Machine "Soft Machine") which later became the Soft Machine formed in 1963,[[7]](#cite_note-7) while the jazz label [Verve](/wiki/Verve_Records "Verve Records") released the influential debut album, *[Freak Out!](/wiki/Freak_Out! "Freak Out!")*, by artist and composer [Frank Zappa](/wiki/Frank_Zappa "Frank Zappa") in 1966.[[8]](#cite_note-OxMilkowski-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Larry_Coryell_1979.jpg/250px-Larry_Coryell_1979.jpg)](/wiki/File:Larry_Coryell_1979.jpg)

Guitarist [Larry Coryell](/wiki/Larry_Coryell "Larry Coryell")

Guitarist [Larry Coryell](/wiki/Larry_Coryell "Larry Coryell"), sometimes called the godfather of fusion, referred to a generation of musicians who had grown up on rock and roll when he said, "We loved Miles but we also loved the [Rolling Stones](/wiki/Rolling_Stones "Rolling Stones")."[[8]](#cite_note-OxMilkowski-8) In 1966, he started the band [the Free Spirits](/wiki/The_Free_Spirits "The Free Spirits") with [Bob Moses](/wiki/Bob_Moses_(musician) "Bob Moses (musician)") on drums and recorded the band's first album,[[5]](#cite_note-Fusions-5) *[Out of Sight and Sound](/wiki/Out_of_Sight_and_Sound "Out of Sight and Sound")*, released in 1967. That same year, *DownBeat* began to report on rock music.[[8]](#cite_note-OxMilkowski-8) After the Free Spirits, Coryell was part of a quartet led by vibraphonist [Gary Burton](/wiki/Gary_Burton "Gary Burton"), releasing the album *[Duster](/wiki/Duster_(Gary_Burton_album) "Duster (Gary Burton album)")* with its rock guitar influence.[[5]](#cite_note-Fusions-5) Burton produced the album *Tomorrow Never Knows* for Count's Jam Band, which included Coryell, [Mike Nock](/wiki/Mike_Nock "Mike Nock"), and [Steve Marcus](/wiki/Steve_Marcus "Steve Marcus"), all of them former students at Berklee College in Boston.[[5]](#cite_note-Fusions-5)

The pioneers of fusion emphasized exploration, energy, electricity, intensity, virtuosity, and volume. [Charles Lloyd](/wiki/Charles_Lloyd_(jazz_musician) "Charles Lloyd (jazz musician)") played a combination of rock and jazz at the [Monterey Jazz Festival](/wiki/Monterey_Jazz_Festival "Monterey Jazz Festival") in 1966 with a quartet that included [Keith Jarrett](/wiki/Keith_Jarrett "Keith Jarrett") and [Jack DeJohnette](/wiki/Jack_DeJohnette "Jack DeJohnette").[[8]](#cite_note-OxMilkowski-8) Lloyd adopted the trappings of the California [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock") scene by playing at the rock venue the [Fillmore West](/wiki/Fillmore_West "Fillmore West"), wearing colorful clothes, and giving his albums titles like *Dream Weaver* and *Forest Flower*, which were bestselling jazz albums in 1967.[[5]](#cite_note-Fusions-5) Flautist [Jeremy Steig](/wiki/Jeremy_Steig "Jeremy Steig") experimented with jazz in his band Jeremy & the Satyrs with vibraphonist [Mike Mainieri](/wiki/Mike_Mainieri "Mike Mainieri"). [Rahsaan Roland Kirk](/wiki/Rahsaan_Roland_Kirk "Rahsaan Roland Kirk") performed with Jimi Hendrix at [Ronnie Scott's Jazz Club](/wiki/Ronnie_Scott%27s_Jazz_Club "Ronnie Scott's Jazz Club") in London.[[8]](#cite_note-OxMilkowski-8)

According to music journalist Zaid Mudhaffer, the term "jazz fusion" was coined in a review of *[Song of Innocence](/wiki/Song_of_Innocence "Song of Innocence")* by [David Axelrod](/wiki/David_Axelrod_(musician) "David Axelrod (musician)") when it was released in 1968.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Axelrod said Davis had played the album before conceiving *[Bitches Brew](/wiki/Bitches_Brew "Bitches Brew")*.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Miles Davis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Miles Davis")]

[Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis") wrote in his autobiography that in 1968 and 1969, he had been listening to [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix"), [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown "James Brown"), and [Sly and the Family Stone](/wiki/Sly_and_the_Family_Stone "Sly and the Family Stone").[[8]](#cite_note-OxMilkowski-8) As members of Davis' band, [Chick Corea](/wiki/Chick_Corea "Chick Corea") and [Herbie Hancock](/wiki/Herbie_Hancock "Herbie Hancock") played electric piano on *[Filles de Kilimanjaro](/wiki/Filles_de_Kilimanjaro "Filles de Kilimanjaro")*. Davis's 1969 album *[In a Silent Way](/wiki/In_a_Silent_Way "In a Silent Way")* is considered his first fusion album.[[11]](#cite_note-Southall-11) Composed of two side-long improvised suites edited heavily by Teo Macero, the album was made by pioneers of jazz fusion: Corea, Hancock, [Tony Williams](/wiki/Tony_Williams_(drummer) "Tony Williams (drummer)"), [Wayne Shorter](/wiki/Wayne_Shorter "Wayne Shorter"), [Joe Zawinul](/wiki/Joe_Zawinul "Joe Zawinul") and [John McLaughlin](/wiki/John_McLaughlin_(musician) "John McLaughlin (musician)"). When Davis recorded *[Bitches Brew](/wiki/Bitches_Brew "Bitches Brew")* in 1969, he mostly abandoned the swing beat in favor of a rock and roll backbeat and bass guitar grooves. The album "mixed free jazz blowing by a large ensemble with electronic keyboards and guitar, plus a dense mix of percussion".[[12]](#cite_note-12) Davis played his trumpet like an electric guitar—plugged in to electronic effects and pedals. By the end of the first year, *Bitches Brew* sold 400,000 copies, four times the average for a Miles Davis album.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Miles_Davis_24.jpg/250px-Miles_Davis_24.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miles_Davis_24.jpg)

Trumpet player [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis") was a key figure in the development of fusion.

Although *Bitches Brew* gave him a [gold record](/wiki/Music_recording_sales_certification "Music recording sales certification"), the use of electric instruments and rock beats created consternation among some jazz critics, who accused Davis of betraying the essence of jazz.[[13]](#cite_note-considine-13) Music critic Kevin Fellezs commented that some members of the jazz community regarded rock music as less sophisticated and more commercial than jazz.[[14]](#cite_note-Briley-14) Over the next two years, the aloof Davis recorded more often, worked with many sidemen, appeared on television, and performed at rock venues. Just as quickly, Davis tested the loyalty of rock fans by continuing to experiment with rock, [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), and electronic treatments. His producer, [Teo Macero](/wiki/Teo_Macero "Teo Macero"), inserted previously recorded material into the [*Jack Johnson* soundtrack](/wiki/Jack_Johnson_(album) "Jack Johnson (album)"), *[Live-Evil](/wiki/Live-Evil_(Miles_Davis_album) "Live-Evil (Miles Davis album)")*, and *[On the Corner](/wiki/On_the_Corner "On the Corner")*.[[15]](#cite_note-Gioia_history-15) *Jack Johnson* (1971) has been cited as "the purest electric jazz record ever made" and "one of the most remarkable jazz rock discs of the era".[[16]](#cite_note-tribute-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Fordham_tribute-17)

### Other musicians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Other musicians")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/JohnMcLaughlin.jpg/250px-JohnMcLaughlin.jpg)](/wiki/File:JohnMcLaughlin.jpg)

[John McLaughlin](/wiki/John_McLaughlin_(musician) "John McLaughlin (musician)") performs during his [Mahavishnu Orchestra](/wiki/Mahavishnu_Orchestra "Mahavishnu Orchestra") period

Herbie Hancock brought elements of funk, disco, and electronic music into commercially successful albums such as *[Head Hunters](/wiki/Head_Hunters "Head Hunters")* (1973) and *[Feets, Don't Fail Me Now](/wiki/Feets,_Don%27t_Fail_Me_Now "Feets, Don't Fail Me Now")* (1979). Several years after recording *[Miles in the Sky](/wiki/Miles_in_the_Sky "Miles in the Sky")* with Davis, guitarist [George Benson](/wiki/George_Benson "George Benson") became a vocalist with enough pop hits to overshadow his earlier career in jazz.[[15]](#cite_note-Gioia_history-15)

While Davis was sidelined, Chick Corea gained prominence. In the early 1970s Corea combined jazz, rock, pop, and Brazilian music in [Return to Forever](/wiki/Return_to_Forever "Return to Forever"), a band that included [Stanley Clarke](/wiki/Stanley_Clarke "Stanley Clarke") on bass guitar and [Al Di Meola](/wiki/Al_Di_Meola "Al Di Meola") on electric guitar. Corea divided the rest of his career between acoustic and electric music, non-commercial and commercial, jazz and pop rock, with a band for each: the Akoustic Band and the Elektric Band.[[15]](#cite_note-Gioia_history-15)

Joe Zawinul and Wayne Shorter started the influential jazz fusion band [Weather Report](/wiki/Weather_Report "Weather Report") in December 1970.[[18]](#cite_note-BKGrove-18) They had a successful career along with major musicians like [Alphonse Mouzon](/wiki/Alphonse_Mouzon "Alphonse Mouzon"), [Jaco Pastorius](/wiki/Jaco_Pastorius "Jaco Pastorius"), [Airto Moreira](/wiki/Airto_Moreira "Airto Moreira") and [Miroslav Vitouš](/wiki/Miroslav_Vitou%C5%A1 "Miroslav Vitouš") until 1986.[[18]](#cite_note-BKGrove-18)

Tony Williams was a member of Davis's band since 1963. Williams reflected, "I wanted to create a different atmosphere from the one I had been in...What better way to do it than to go electric?" He left Davis to form [the Tony Williams Lifetime](/wiki/The_Tony_Williams_Lifetime "The Tony Williams Lifetime") with English guitarist John McLaughlin and organist [Larry Young](/wiki/Larry_Young_(musician) "Larry Young (musician)"). The band combined rock intensity and loudness with jazz spontaneity. The debut album [*Emergency!*](/wiki/Emergency!_(album) "Emergency! (album)") was recorded three months before *Bitches Brew*.[[8]](#cite_note-OxMilkowski-8)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Although McLaughlin had worked with Miles Davis, he was influenced more by Jimi Hendrix and had played with English rock musicians [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton") and [Mick Jagger](/wiki/Mick_Jagger "Mick Jagger") before creating the Mahavishnu Orchestra around the same time that Corea started Return to Forever. McLaughlin had been a member of Tony Williams's Lifetime. He brought to his music many of the elements that interested other musicians in the 1960s and early 1970s: counterculture, rock and roll, electronic instruments, solo virtuosity, experimentation, the blending of genres, and an interest in the exotic, such as Indian music.[[15]](#cite_note-Gioia_history-15) He formed the [Mahavishnu Orchestra](/wiki/Mahavishnu_Orchestra "Mahavishnu Orchestra") with drummer [Billy Cobham](/wiki/Billy_Cobham "Billy Cobham"), violinist [Jerry Goodman](/wiki/Jerry_Goodman "Jerry Goodman"), bassist [Rick Laird](/wiki/Rick_Laird "Rick Laird"), and keyboardist [Jan Hammer](/wiki/Jan_Hammer "Jan Hammer"). The band released its first album, *[The Inner Mounting Flame](/wiki/The_Inner_Mounting_Flame "The Inner Mounting Flame")*, in 1971. Hammer pioneered the use of the [Minimoog](/wiki/Minimoog "Minimoog") synthesizer with distortion effects. His use of the pitch bend wheel made a keyboard sound like an electric guitar. The Mahavishnu Orchestra was influenced by both psychedelic rock and [Indian classical music](/wiki/Indian_classical_music "Indian classical music"). The band's first lineup broke up after two studio albums and one live album, but McLaughlin formed another group in 1974 under the same name with jazz violinist [Jean-Luc Ponty](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Ponty "Jean-Luc Ponty"), one of the first electric violinists.

After leaving the Mahavishnu Orchestra in 1975 Jean-Luc Ponty signed with [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records") and released number of successful jazz fusion solo albums that entered top 5 of the [*Billboard* jazz charts](/wiki/Top_Jazz_Albums "Top Jazz Albums") in mid '70s — '80s.

During the late 1970s, [Lee Ritenour](/wiki/Lee_Ritenour "Lee Ritenour"), [Stuff](/wiki/Stuff_(band) "Stuff (band)"), [George Benson](/wiki/George_Benson "George Benson"), [Chuck Mangione](/wiki/Chuck_Mangione "Chuck Mangione"), [Spyro Gyra](/wiki/Spyro_Gyra "Spyro Gyra"), [the Crusaders](/wiki/The_Crusaders_(jazz_fusion_group) "The Crusaders (jazz fusion group)"), and [Larry Carlton](/wiki/Larry_Carlton "Larry Carlton")[[21]](#cite_note-21) released fusion albums. Early 1980s, Larsen Feiten band, [George Benson](/wiki/George_Benson "George Benson"), [Lee Ritenour](/wiki/Lee_Ritenour "Lee Ritenour"), [Al Jarreau](/wiki/Al_Jarreau "Al Jarreau"), and the Clark/Duke Project gained pop hits.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Jazz rock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Jazz rock")]

| Jazz rock | |
| --- | --- |
| Stylistic origins | * Jazz * rock * jazz fusion |
| Typical instruments | * Electric guitar * piano * [electric piano](/wiki/Electric_piano "Electric piano") * drums * saxophone * trumpet * [electronic keyboard](/wiki/Electronic_keyboard "Electronic keyboard") * bass guitar * vocals |
| Derivative forms | * [Jam band](/wiki/Jam_band "Jam band") * [punk jazz](/wiki/Punk_jazz "Punk jazz") * [jazz metal](/wiki/Jazz_metal "Jazz metal") * [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock") * [krautrock](/wiki/Krautrock "Krautrock") * [yacht rock](/wiki/Yacht_rock "Yacht rock") |

The term "**jazz rock**" (or "jazz/rock") is sometimes used as a synonym for "jazz fusion". [The Free Spirits](/wiki/The_Free_Spirits "The Free Spirits") have sometimes been cited as the earliest jazz rock band.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Jazz rock bands such as [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_(band) "Chicago (band)"), [The Ides of March](/wiki/The_Ides_of_March_(band) "The Ides of March (band)"), [Blood, Sweat & Tears](/wiki/Blood_Sweat_%26_Tears "Blood Sweat & Tears"), [Chase](/wiki/Chase_(band) "Chase (band)"), [Lighthouse](/wiki/Lighthouse_(band) "Lighthouse (band)"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Santana](/wiki/Santana_(band) "Santana (band)"),[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Colosseum](/wiki/Colosseum_(band) "Colosseum (band)"), [Soft Machine](/wiki/Soft_Machine "Soft Machine"), [Nucleus](/wiki/Nucleus_(band) "Nucleus (band)"), [Brand X](/wiki/Brand_X "Brand X"), [the Mothers of Invention](/wiki/The_Mothers_of_Invention "The Mothers of Invention") and [IF](/wiki/If_(band) "If (band)") blended jazz and rock with electric instruments.[[26]](#cite_note-tesser-26) Miles Davis' fusion jazz was "pure melody and tonal color",[[26]](#cite_note-tesser-26) while Frank Zappa's music was more "complex" and "unpredictable".[[27]](#cite_note-AMGJ2-27) Zappa released the solo album *[Hot Rats](/wiki/Hot_Rats "Hot Rats")* in 1969.[[28]](#cite_note-Huey-28) The album contained long instrumental pieces with a jazz influence.[[29]](#cite_note-mileshotrats-29)[[30]](#cite_note-hotrats-30) Zappa released two albums, *[Waka/Jawaka](/wiki/Waka/Jawaka "Waka/Jawaka")* and *[The Grand Wazoo](/wiki/The_Grand_Wazoo "The Grand Wazoo")*, in 1972, which were influenced by jazz. [George Duke](/wiki/George_Duke "George Duke") and [Aynsley Dunbar](/wiki/Aynsley_Dunbar "Aynsley Dunbar") played on both. 1970s band [Steely Dan](/wiki/Steely_Dan "Steely Dan") has been lauded by music critic [Neil McCormick](/wiki/Neil_McCormick "Neil McCormick") for their "smooth, smart jazz-rock fusion".[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The jazz artists of the 1960s and 1970s had a large impact on many rock groups of that era such as [Santana](/wiki/Santana_(band) "Santana (band)") and Frank Zappa. They took jazz phrasing and harmony and incorporated it into modern rock music, significantly changing music history and paving the way for artists that would follow in their footsteps. Carlos Santana in particular has given much credit to Miles Davis and the influence he had on his music. While Miles Davis combined jazz with modal and rock influences, [Carlos Santana](/wiki/Carlos_Santana "Carlos Santana") combined these along with Latin rhythms and feel, shaping a whole new genre, [Latin rock](/wiki/Latin_rock "Latin rock").

According to AllMusic, the term jazz rock "may refer to the loudest, wildest, most electrified fusion bands from the jazz camp, but most often it describes performers coming from the rock side of the equation...jazz rock first emerged during the late '60s as an attempt to fuse the visceral power of rock with the musical complexity and improvisational fireworks of jazz. Since rock often emphasized directness and simplicity over virtuosity, jazz rock generally grew out of the most artistically ambitious rock subgenres of the late '60s and early '70s: [psychedelia](/wiki/Psychedelic_music "Psychedelic music"), [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock"), and the singer-songwriter movement."[[32]](#cite_note-JR_Overview-32)

According to jazz writer Stuart Nicholson, jazz rock paralleled [free jazz](/wiki/Free_jazz "Free jazz") by being "on the verge of creating a whole new musical language in the 1960s". He said the albums *[Emergency!](/wiki/Emergency!_(album) "Emergency! (album)")* (1969) by the [Tony Williams Lifetime](/wiki/Tony_Williams_Lifetime "Tony Williams Lifetime") and *[Agharta](/wiki/Agharta_(album) "Agharta (album)")* (1975) by Miles Davis "suggested the potential of evolving into something that might eventually define itself as a wholly independent genre quite apart from the sound and conventions of anything that had gone before". This development was stifled by commercialism, Nicholson said, as the genre "mutated into a peculiar species of jazz-inflected pop music that eventually took up residence on FM radio" at the end of the 1970s.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In the 1970s, American fusion was being combined in the U.K. with progressive rock and psychedelic music. Bands who were part of this movement included Brand X (with [Phil Collins](/wiki/Phil_Collins "Phil Collins") of Genesis), Bruford ([Bill Bruford](/wiki/Bill_Bruford "Bill Bruford") of Yes), and Nucleus (led by [Ian Carr](/wiki/Ian_Carr "Ian Carr")). Throughout Europe and the world this movement grew due to the efforts of [Magma](/wiki/Magma_(band) "Magma (band)") in France, [Passport](/wiki/Passport_(band) "Passport (band)") in Germany, guitarist [Jan Akkerman](/wiki/Jan_Akkerman "Jan Akkerman") of the Netherlands, [Terje Rypdal](/wiki/Terje_Rypdal "Terje Rypdal") in Norway, [Jukka Tolonen](/wiki/Jukka_Tolonen "Jukka Tolonen") of Finland, and the Japanese guitarists [Ryo Kawasaki](/wiki/Ryo_Kawasaki "Ryo Kawasaki") and [Kazumi Watanabe](/wiki/Kazumi_Watanabe "Kazumi Watanabe").[[8]](#cite_note-OxMilkowski-8)

**Jazz metal** is the fusion of jazz fusion and jazz rock with [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music"). [Animals as Leaders](/wiki/Animals_as_Leaders "Animals as Leaders")' albums *[The Joy of Motion](/wiki/The_Joy_of_Motion "The Joy of Motion")* (2014) and *[The Madness of Many](/wiki/The_Madness_of_Many "The Madness of Many")* (2016) have been described as progressive metal combined with jazz fusion.[[34]](#cite_note-34) [Panzerballett](/wiki/Panzerballett "Panzerballett") blends jazz with heavy metal.[[35]](#cite_note-panzerballett-35) [Imperial Triumphant](/wiki/Imperial_Triumphant "Imperial Triumphant") combines jazz with [death metal](/wiki/Death_metal "Death metal") and [black metal](/wiki/Black_metal "Black metal").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Jazz pop

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Jazz pop")]

| Jazz-pop | |
| --- | --- |
| Stylistic origins | * [Vocal jazz](/wiki/Vocal_jazz "Vocal jazz") * [Traditional Pop](/wiki/Traditional_Pop "Traditional Pop") * [Swing](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music") * [Rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") * [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") * [bossa nova](/wiki/Bossa_nova "Bossa nova") * [latin jazz](/wiki/Latin_jazz "Latin jazz") * [easy listening](/wiki/Easy_listening "Easy listening") * [lounge music](/wiki/Lounge_music "Lounge music") |
| Cultural origins | 1960s, United States |
| Derivative forms | [Smooth jazz](/wiki/Smooth_jazz "Smooth jazz") |

**Jazz pop** (or **pop-jazz**, also called **jazzy pop**) is music with jazz instruments and soft production that is commercially viable and radio-friendly.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In jazz pop, the music has less improvisation, but retains the melody and swing of jazz.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Robert Palmer from *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* cited that jazz pop should be distinguished from [jazz rock](/wiki/Jazz_rock "Jazz rock").[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Examples of jazz-pop musicians are [Kenny G](/wiki/Kenny_G "Kenny G"), [Bob James](/wiki/Bob_James_(musician) "Bob James (musician)"), and [George Benson](/wiki/George_Benson "George Benson"). [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") has been referred to as a vocalist whose style blended jazz influences with [traditional pop](/wiki/Traditional_pop "Traditional pop") and [crooning](/wiki/Crooner "Crooner").[[40]](#cite_note-40) A more recent example of a vocalist described as jazz-pop is [Laufey](/wiki/Laufey_(singer) "Laufey (singer)").[[41]](#cite_note-nprtradpop-41)[[42]](#cite_note-nytpop-42)

## Smooth jazz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Smooth jazz")]

Main article: [Smooth jazz](/wiki/Smooth_jazz "Smooth jazz")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Spyro_Gyra_-_jazz_band.JPG/250px-Spyro_Gyra_-_jazz_band.JPG)](/wiki/File:Spyro_Gyra_-_jazz_band.JPG)

[Spyro Gyra](/wiki/Spyro_Gyra "Spyro Gyra") combines jazz with R&B, funk and pop.

By the early 1980s, much of the original fusion genre was subsumed into other branches of jazz and rock, especially [smooth jazz](/wiki/Smooth_jazz "Smooth jazz"), a radio-friendly subgenre of fusion which is influenced by R&B, funk, and pop music.[[43]](#cite_note-WhatIsSmoothJazz-43) Smooth jazz can be traced to at least the late 1960s, when producer [Creed Taylor](/wiki/Creed_Taylor "Creed Taylor") worked with guitarist [Wes Montgomery](/wiki/Wes_Montgomery "Wes Montgomery") on three popular music-oriented albums. Taylor founded [CTI Records](/wiki/CTI_Records "CTI Records") and many established jazz performers recorded for CTI, including [Freddie Hubbard](/wiki/Freddie_Hubbard "Freddie Hubbard"), [Chet Baker](/wiki/Chet_Baker "Chet Baker"), George Benson, and [Stanley Turrentine](/wiki/Stanley_Turrentine "Stanley Turrentine"). Albums under Taylor's guidance were aimed at both pop and jazz fans.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The merging of jazz and pop/rock music took a more commercial direction in the late 1970s and early 1980s, in the form of compositions with a softer sound palette that could fit comfortably in a [soft rock](/wiki/Soft_rock "Soft rock") radio playlist. The AllMusic guide's article on fusion states that "unfortunately, as it became a money-maker and as rock declined artistically from the mid-'70s on, much of what was labeled fusion was actually a combination of jazz with easy-listening pop music and lightweight R&B."[[6]](#cite_note-Fusion_overview-6)

[Michael](/wiki/Michael_Brecker "Michael Brecker") and [Randy Brecker](/wiki/Randy_Brecker "Randy Brecker") produced funk-influenced jazz with soloists.[[44]](#cite_note-Lawn-44) David Sanborn was considered a "soulful" and "influential" voice.[[44]](#cite_note-Lawn-44) However, Kenny G was criticized by both fusion and jazz fans, and some musicians, while having become a huge commercial success. Music reviewer George Graham argues that the "so-called 'smooth jazz' sound of people like Kenny G has none of the fire and creativity that marked the best of the fusion scene during its heyday in the 1970s."[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Other styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other styles")]

### Punk jazz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Punk jazz")]

Main article: [Punk jazz](/wiki/Punk_jazz "Punk jazz")

In the 1990s, another kind of fusion took a more hardcore approach. [Bill Laswell](/wiki/Bill_Laswell "Bill Laswell") produced many albums in this movement, such as *Ask the Ages* by avant-garde guitarist Sonny Sharrock and *[Arc of the Testimony](/wiki/Arc_of_the_Testimony "Arc of the Testimony")* with Laswell's band [Arcana](/wiki/Arcana_(American_band) "Arcana (American band)"). [Niacin](/wiki/Niacin_(band) "Niacin (band)") was formed by rock bassist Billy Sheehan, drummer Dennis Chambers, and organist John Novello.[[8]](#cite_note-OxMilkowski-8)

In London, [The Pop Group](/wiki/The_Pop_Group "The Pop Group") began to mix free jazz and reggae into their form of punk rock.[[46]](#cite_note-Lang-46) In New York City, [no wave](/wiki/No_wave "No wave") was inspired by free jazz and punk. Examples of this style include [Lydia Lunch](/wiki/Lydia_Lunch "Lydia Lunch")'s *Queen of Siam*,[[47]](#cite_note-Bangs-47) [James Chance and the Contortions](/wiki/James_Chance_and_the_Contortions "James Chance and the Contortions"), who mixed soul music with free jazz and punk rock, and the [Lounge Lizards](/wiki/Lounge_Lizards "Lounge Lizards"),[[47]](#cite_note-Bangs-47) the first group to call themselves [punk jazz](/wiki/Punk_jazz "Punk jazz").[[47]](#cite_note-Bangs-47)

[John Zorn](/wiki/John_Zorn "John Zorn") took note of the emphasis on speed and dissonance that was becoming prevalent in punk rock and incorporated them into free jazz with the release of the *[Spy vs Spy](/wiki/Spy_vs_Spy_(album) "Spy vs Spy (album)")* album in 1986. The album was a collection of [Ornette Coleman](/wiki/Ornette_Coleman "Ornette Coleman") tunes played in the [thrashcore](/wiki/Thrashcore "Thrashcore") style.[[48]](#cite_note-48) In the same year, [Sonny Sharrock](/wiki/Sonny_Sharrock "Sonny Sharrock"), [Peter Brötzmann](/wiki/Peter_Br%C3%B6tzmann "Peter Brötzmann"), Bill Laswell, and [Ronald Shannon Jackson](/wiki/Ronald_Shannon_Jackson "Ronald Shannon Jackson") recorded the first album under the name [Last Exit](/wiki/Last_Exit_(free_jazz_band) "Last Exit (free jazz band)"), a blend of thrash and free jazz.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### Jazz-funk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Jazz-funk")]

Main article: [Jazz-funk](/wiki/Jazz-funk "Jazz-funk")

Jazz-funk is characterized by a strong [back beat](/wiki/Beat_(music) "Beat (music)") ([groove](/wiki/Groove_(music) "Groove (music)")), electrified sounds,[[50]](#cite_note-50) and an early prevalence of [analog synthesizers](/wiki/Analog_synthesizers "Analog synthesizers"). The integration of [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), and [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") music and styles into jazz resulted in the creation of a genre whose spectrum is quite wide and ranges from strong [jazz improvisation](/wiki/Musical_improvisation#Jazz_improvisation "Musical improvisation") to soul, funk or [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") with jazz arrangements, jazz [riffs](/wiki/Riff "Riff"), jazz solos, and sometimes soul vocals.[[51]](#cite_note-allmusic-51)

Jazz-funk is primarily an American genre, where it was popular throughout the 1970s and the early 1980s, but it also achieved noted appeal on the club-circuit in England during the mid-1970s. Jazz-funk retains a stronger feel of groove and R&B versus some of the jazz fusion production, and is more arranged and features more improvisation than [soul jazz](/wiki/Soul_jazz "Soul jazz").

### M-Base

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: M-Base")]

Main article: [M-Base](/wiki/M-Base "M-Base")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Steve_Coleman_1611.JPG/250px-Steve_Coleman_1611.JPG)](/wiki/File:Steve_Coleman_1611.JPG)

Steve Coleman in Paris, July 2004

M-Base ("macro-basic array of structured extemporization") centers on a movement started in the 1980s. It started as a group of young African-American musicians in New York which included [Steve Coleman](/wiki/Steve_Coleman "Steve Coleman"), [Greg Osby](/wiki/Greg_Osby "Greg Osby"), and [Gary Thomas](/wiki/Gary_Thomas_(musician) "Gary Thomas (musician)") developing a complex but grooving sound.[[52]](#cite_note-Jost-52) In the 1990s most M-Base participants turned to more conventional music, but Coleman, the most active participant, continued developing his music in accordance with the M-Base concept.[[53]](#cite_note-Blumenfeld-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Undead-54) M-Base changed from a loose collective to an informal "school".[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Afro-Cuban jazz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Afro-Cuban jazz")]

Main article: [Afro-Cuban jazz](/wiki/Afro-Cuban_jazz "Afro-Cuban jazz")

Afro-Cuban jazz, one of the earliest forms of [Latin jazz](/wiki/Latin_jazz "Latin jazz"), is a fusion of Afro-Cuban clave-based rhythms with jazz harmonies and techniques of improvisation. Afro-Cuban jazz emerged in the early 1940s with the Cuban musicians [Mario Bauza](/wiki/Mario_Bauza "Mario Bauza") and [Frank Grillo "Machito"](/wiki/Machito "Machito") in the band Machito and his Afro-Cubans in New York City. In 1947 the collaborations of bebop innovator Dizzy Gillespie with Cuban percussionist [Chano Pozo](/wiki/Chano_Pozo "Chano Pozo") brought Afro-Cuban rhythms and instruments, most notably the congas and the bongos, into the East Coast jazz scene. Early combinations of jazz with Cuban music, such as Gillespie's and Pozo's "Manteca" and Charlie Parker's and Machito's "Mangó Mangüé", were commonly referred to as "Cubop", short for Cuban bebop.[[56]](#cite_note-Fernandez2006-56) During its first decades, the Afro-Cuban jazz movement was stronger in the United States than in Cuba.[[57]](#cite_note-Acosta-57)

## Influence on rock music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_fusion&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Influence on rock music")]

See also: [Progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock"), [art rock](/wiki/Art_rock "Art rock"), and [progressive metal](/wiki/Progressive_metal "Progressive metal")

According to bassist [Randy Jackson](/wiki/Randy_Jackson "Randy Jackson"), jazz fusion is a difficult genre to play. "[I] picked jazz fusion because I was trying to become the ultimate technical musician—able to play anything. Jazz fusion to me is the hardest music to play. You have to be so proficient on your instrument. Playing five tempos at the same time, for instance. I wanted to try the toughest music because I knew if I could do that, I could do anything."[[58]](#cite_note-JacksonBaker2004-58)

[Progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock"), with its affinity for long solos, diverse influences, non-standard time signatures, and complex music had very similar musical values as jazz fusion. Some prominent examples of progressive rock mixed with elements of fusion is the music of [Gong](/wiki/Gong_(band) "Gong (band)"), [King Crimson](/wiki/King_Crimson "King Crimson"), [Ozric Tentacles](/wiki/Ozric_Tentacles "Ozric Tentacles"), and [Emerson, Lake & Palmer](/wiki/Emerson,_Lake_%26_Palmer "Emerson, Lake & Palmer").

Jazz rock fusion's technically challenging guitar solos, bass solos, and odd-metered, syncopated drumming started to be incorporated in the technically focused [progressive metal](/wiki/Progressive_metal "Progressive metal") genre by the late 1980s. [Watchtower](/wiki/Watchtower_(band) "Watchtower (band)")'s 1989 album *[Control and Resistance](/wiki/Control_and_Resistance "Control and Resistance")* is one of the earliest progressive/[thrash metal](/wiki/Thrash_metal "Thrash metal") albums to experiment with a jazz fusion-influenced sound.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The [death metal](/wiki/Death_metal "Death metal") band [Atheist](/wiki/Atheist_(band) "Atheist (band)") produced albums *[Unquestionable Presence](/wiki/Unquestionable_Presence "Unquestionable Presence")* in 1991 and *[Elements](/wiki/Elements_(Atheist_album) "Elements (Atheist album)")* in 1993 containing heavily syncopated drumming, changing time signatures, instrumental parts, acoustic interludes, and Latin rhythms. [Meshuggah](/wiki/Meshuggah "Meshuggah") first attracted international attention with the 1995 release *[Destroy Erase Improve](/wiki/Destroy_Erase_Improve "Destroy Erase Improve")* for its fusion of fast-tempo death metal, thrash metal, and progressive metal with jazz fusion elements. [Cynic](/wiki/Cynic_(band) "Cynic (band)") recorded a complex, unorthodox form of jazz fusion influenced experimental death metal with their 1993 album *[Focus](/wiki/Focus_(Cynic_album) "Focus (Cynic album)")*. In 1997, Guitar Institute of Technology guitarist [Jennifer Batten](/wiki/Jennifer_Batten "Jennifer Batten") under the name of [Jennifer Batten's Tribal Rage: Momentum](/wiki/Jennifer_Batten%27s_Tribal_Rage:_Momentum "Jennifer Batten's Tribal Rage: Momentum") released *Momentum*—an instrumental hybrid of rock, fusion, and exotic sounds. [Mudvayne](/wiki/Mudvayne "Mudvayne") is heavily influenced by jazz, especially in bassist [Ryan Martinie](/wiki/Ryan_Martinie "Ryan Martinie")'s playing.[[60]](#cite_note-Ratliff-60)[[61]](#cite_note-Rvr-61) [Puya](/wiki/Puya_(band) "Puya (band)") frequently incorporates influences from American and [Latin jazz](/wiki/Latin_jazz "Latin jazz") music.[[62]](#cite_note-Mateus-62)

Another, more cerebral, all-instrumental progressive jazz fusion-metal band [Planet X](/wiki/Planet_X_(band) "Planet X (band)") released *[Universe](/wiki/Universe_(Planet_X_album) "Universe (Planet X album)")* in 2000 with [Tony MacAlpine](/wiki/Tony_MacAlpine "Tony MacAlpine"), [Derek Sherinian](/wiki/Derek_Sherinian "Derek Sherinian") (ex-[Dream Theater](/wiki/Dream_Theater "Dream Theater")), and [Virgil Donati](/wiki/Virgil_Donati "Virgil Donati") (who has played with [Scott Henderson](/wiki/Scott_Henderson "Scott Henderson") from [Tribal Tech](/wiki/Tribal_Tech "Tribal Tech")). The band blends fusion-style guitar solos and syncopated odd-metered drumming with the heaviness of metal. Tech-prog-fusion metal band [Aghora](/wiki/Aghora_(band) "Aghora (band)") formed in 1995 and released their first album, self-titled *[Aghora](/wiki/Aghora_(album) "Aghora (album)")*, recorded in 1999 with [Sean Malone](/wiki/Sean_Malone "Sean Malone") and [Sean Reinert](/wiki/Sean_Reinert "Sean Reinert"), both former members of Cynic. [Gordian Knot](/wiki/Gordian_Knot_(band) "Gordian Knot (band)"), another Cynic-linked experimental progressive metal band, released its debut album in 1999 which explored a range of styles from jazz fusion to metal. [The Mars Volta](/wiki/The_Mars_Volta "The Mars Volta") is extremely influenced by jazz fusion, using progressive, unexpected turns in the drum patterns and instrumental lines.
